title: Intermittent 403 Forbidden on dashboard for users with new 'analyst' role after recent deployment
problem: Users are receiving 403 Forbidden errors on approximately 1 in 3 fresh page loads of the TaskFlow dashboard. The issue began shortly after a deployment that introduced a new 'analyst' role. Multiple team members are affected, and at least the confirmed cases have the new 'analyst' role assigned.
root_cause_hypothesis: The recent deployment added the 'analyst' role but its dashboard access permissions were not consistently applied across all backend instances. With multiple servers behind a load balancer, requests routed to an instance missing the 'analyst' role's authorization grant return 403, while instances with the correct configuration serve the page normally. This explains both the intermittent pattern and the ~1-in-3 failure rate.
reproduction_steps: Ensure the user account has the 'analyst' role assigned
Navigate to the TaskFlow dashboard URL
Refresh the page repeatedly (roughly 1 in 3 loads should return 403)
Note: the issue is on fresh page loads, not mid-session
environment: TaskFlow dashboard, post-deployment that introduced the 'analyst' role (approximately 2 days before report). Multiple users affected. No specific browser/OS constraints reported — issue appears server-side.
impact: Users with the 'analyst' role are intermittently locked out of the dashboard. This blocks their workflow roughly a third of the time and affects multiple team members. The inconsistency also erodes user trust in the application's reliability.
recommended_fix: 1. Compare the authorization/role-permission configuration across all backend instances serving the dashboard — look for instances that do not grant dashboard access to the 'analyst' role. 2. Check the deployment rollout: verify whether all instances received the role configuration update, or if some are running a stale config. 3. Ensure the 'analyst' role has an explicit 'allow' entry for the dashboard route in the permissions matrix. 4. Redeploy or sync configuration across all instances and verify consistent behavior.
proposed_test_case: Assign a test user the 'analyst' role and make 20+ sequential requests to the dashboard endpoint, recording which backend instance handles each request (via response headers or logs). Verify that all instances return 200 and none return 403. Additionally, add an integration test that asserts all defined roles (including 'analyst') have the expected permissions for core routes like the dashboard.
information_gaps: Whether any users without the 'analyst' role are also affected (reporter was uncertain — developer can verify via access logs)
Exact number of backend instances and which specific ones may be misconfigured
Whether the role permission config is stored in code, a database, or an external auth service
